coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of yellow-green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles and their application in detection of nitrophenol isomers
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yangxia
Wang, Yuxiang
Zhang, Haixia
Zhao, Liang
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: A clear formation mechanism is essential for the controllable synthesis of nanomaterials with different optical properties, which is also one of the challenges facing the preparation of fluorescent silicon nanomaterials. In this work, a one-step room temperature synthesis method was established to prepare yellow-green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs). The obtained SiNPs exhibited excellent pH stability, salt tolerance, anti-photobleaching ability and biocompatibility. Based on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, ultra high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry and other characterization data, the formation mechanism of the SiNPs was proposed, which provided a theoretical basis and important reference for the controllable preparation of SiNPs and other fluorescent nanomaterials. In addition, the obtained SiNPs illustrated excellent sensitivity for nitrophenol isomers, the linear range of o-nitrophenol, m-nitrophenol, p-nitrophenol was 0.05–600 μM, 20–600 μM and 0.01–600 μM under the λ ex and λ em were set as 440 nm and 549 nm, and related limit detection was 16.7 nM, 6.7 μM and 3.3 nM, respectively. The developed SiNP-based sensor achieved satisfactory recoveries in detecting nitrophenol isomers in a river water sample, showing great promise in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanoparticles
Fluorescence sensor
Room temperature synthesis
Mechanism
Nitrophenol isomers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and the formation mechanism of the SiNPs
Selectivity and sensitivity of the SiNPs to NPs isomers
Investigation on the detection mechanism of NPs isomers by SiNPs
Cell activity and bioimaging of the SiNPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Gansu Youth Science and Technology Fund Project

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High-resolution XPS fine spectra of (A) C, (B) N, (C) O and (D) Si elements in the SiNPs; (E) FTIR pattern and (F) TEM image (inset was the particle size distribution histogram) of the SiNPs.
Diagram of the formation mechanism of the SiNPs.

The effect of (A, D) 2-NP, (B, E) 3-NP and (C, F) 4-NP on the fluorescence intensity of the SiNPs and the corresponding linear relationship; (G–I) Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F/F 0) of the SiNPs by different substances. (The concentration of 2-NP, 3-NP, 4-NP and others was 500 μM).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of the (A) SiNPs and/or 2-NP, (B) SiNPs and/or 3-NP, (C) SiNPs and/or 4-NP; (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the 2-NP, 3-NP, 4-NP, and the fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of the SiNPs.

(A) The effect of different concentrations of the SiNPs on the viability of Hela cells; (B) Dark field, (C) bright field, and (D) merged images of Hela cells incubated with the 1000 μg/mL SiNPs for 2 h.
Preparation of the SiNPs and determination of NPs isomers.
Comparision of the detection performance with other methods.

row.entry: β-CD involved dual-ligands cofunctionalized Au NCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of NPs isomers in Yellow River water samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of yellow-green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles and their application in detection of nitrophenol isomers

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources/Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A clear formation mechanism is essential for the controllable synthesis of nanomaterials with different optical properties, which is also one of the challenges facing the preparation of fluorescent silicon nanomaterials. In this work, a one-step room temperature synthesis method was established to prepare yellow-green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs). The obtained SiNPs exhibited excellent pH stability, salt tolerance, anti-photobleaching ability and biocompatibility. Based on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, ultra high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry and other characterization data, the formation mechanism of the SiNPs was proposed, which provided a theoretical basis and important reference for the controllable preparation of SiNPs and other fluorescent nanomaterials. In addition, the obtained SiNPs illustrated excellent sensitivity for nitrophenol isomers, the linear range of o-nitrophenol, m-nitrophenol, p-nitrophenol was 0.05–600 μM, 20–600 μM and 0.01–600 μM under the λ ex and λ em were set as 440 nm and 549 nm, and related limit detection was 16.7 nM, 6.7 μM and 3.3 nM, respectively. The developed SiNP-based sensor achieved satisfactory recoveries in detecting nitrophenol isomers in a river water sample, showing great promise in practical applications.

Fluorescent SiNPs with excellent performance were prepared at room temperature reaction. The formation mechanism of the SiNPs was proposed and the obtained SiNPs exhibited excellent performance to nitrophenol isomers detection.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: So far, fluorescent silicon nanomaterials mainly included silicon nanowires, silicon nanorods, silicon quantum dots and silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) [1–4]. Among them, SiNPs have brought about widespread attentions. SiNPs-in contrast to silica nanoparticles-are fluorescent by themselves, they need not be rendered fluorescent by various post-modification methods, such as incorporation of dyes into the interior of the material, or by methods that are comparable to processes that can be used to modify glass surfaces [5]. Therefore, various methods including hydrothermal, microwave, ultraviolet irradiation, room temperature synthesis, etc. have been developed, and SiNPs with different emission bands have been prepared [6–9]. Brilliantly, room temperature synthesis disclosed unique advantages in the preparation of the SiNPs because the preparation conditions were mild and easy to operate, and there was no need for complex surroundings such as pressurization and heating. For any of the methods mentioned above, SiNPs with excellent fluorescence properties have been designed and applied in various fields. However, there were few systematic or comprehensive reports on the formation mechanism of SiNPs [10]. As we all know, clarifying the formation mechanism of nanomaterials has an important guiding role and reference value for the preparation of “tailorable” materials with controllable fluorescence properties, and also emerged a good promotion effect for broadening the application range of nanomaterials.

Reports showed that silicon nanomaterials devour superior optical, physical and electrochemical properties, as well as considerable biocompatibility. Therefore, exploration on silicon nanomaterials in the fields of lithium-ion batteries electrodes, drug delivery, biosensing and bioimaging has caused extensive concerns [11–13]. Among them, biosensing and biological imaging have been extensively studied in recent years [14–16]. The above reports have declared that SiNPs could be excellent candidates for sensor and cell staining agents.

Nitrophenol (NPs) isomers such as o-nitrophenol (2-NP), m-nitrophenol (3-NP), and p-nitrophenol (4-NP), as endocrine disruptors, have been listed as priority water pollutants by the USA Environmental Protection Agency [17,18]. The Commission of the European Communities has also itemized NPs as priority toxic pollutants [19]. NPs isomers are widely used in fine chemicals, pharmaceuticals, pesticides, gasoline, and diesel, and could be discharged into the environment from industrial wastewater or exhaust gas, thus causing toxic effects on plants, animals, and humans [20]. The toxicity of these isomers also showed differences, the 2-NP and 4-NP having more severe toxic side effects to the organism than 3-NP. Generally, human exposure to NPs isomers may generate to many complications, such as endocrine disturbances, antiandrogen effects, methemoglobinemia, and respiratory diseases [21,22]. In view of this, various analytical techniques for the determination of NPs isomers have been developed. The reported methods mainly include electrochemistry [23], high performance liquid chromatography [24], capillary electrophoresis [25]], GC-MS [26], spectrophotometry [27], chemiluminescence [28], spectrofluorimetry [29,30], etc. Among them, fluorescence strategy could meet the requirement of NPs isomers detection and has attracted widespread attention due to its high sensitivity, fast response speed, simple operation, and no need for complicated sample pretreatment steps [31,32].

As a proof-of-concept, the SiNPs emitting yellow-green fluorescence were rapidly prepared with 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride (DAPH) and N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)-propyl]- ethylenediamine (DAMO) as precursors at room temperature in this work (Scheme 1 A). Fluorescence test showed that the SiNPs had excellent pH stability, salt resistance, photobleaching resistance, and biocompatibility. In particular, the SiNP-based fluorescence strategy was established for NPs isomers (2-NP, 3-NP and 4-NP) sensing (Scheme 1B). The detection limit reached as low as 3.3 nM for 4-NP, 16.7 nM for 2-NP, and 6.7 μM for 3-NP. In addition, the formation mechanism of the SiNPs was proposed, which provided important reference value for the preparation of the same type of silicon nanomaterials.

During stirring at room temperature, 15 mg DAPH was added to 5.0 mL of deionized water and fully dissolved, then 1.0 mL of DAMO was added dropwise and the above system was allowed to react at room temperature for 3 h. The obtained SiNPs were filtered with a 0.1 μm filter membrane, and then treated with a dialysis bag with a molecular cut-off of 1000 Da for 4 h. The purified SiNPs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

The obtained 20 μL SiNPs were added to a series of 1.0 mL 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH = 7.0), and then 2-NP (or 3-NP, 4-NP) solution of different concentrations were added, respectively. Thereafter, the above solutions were placed for 1 min to dissolve uniformly and the fluorescence spectra were measured. The working curves were plotted according to log (F
0
/F) (or F
0
/F) and c 2-NP (or c 3-NP, c 4-NP) (F
0 and F represented fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of NPs isomers, respectively). In addition, according to the above methods for the detection of NPs isomers by the SiNPs, the effects of different interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity of the SiNPs were investigated. The detection process was the same as the above-mentioned procedures for the SiNPs detection of NPs isomers, except that NPs isomers were replaced with potential interfering substances.
In our previous report, SiNPs with green- and red-emitting fluorescence were synthesized [10].

When the mass of DAPH increased from 1.0 mg to 25 mg, the fluorescence emission wavelength of SiNPs gradually red-shifted. However, the fluorescence emission wavelength of SiNPs obtained located at 627 nm when DAPH was 30 mg. Therefore, the products formed by the reaction of 1.0 mL DAMO with 1.0 mg and 30 mg were named green SiNPs and red SiNPs, respectively. In this work, the product formed by the reaction of 1.0 mL DAMO and 15 mg DAPH was taken as the representative of yellow-green SiNPs. To ensure the high fluorescence emission performance of SiNPs, the reaction time of DAPH and DAMO was optimized, and high fluorescence intensity was obtained at 3 h (Fig. S1). The quantum yield of the SiNPs was 18%. Subsequently, the optical properties of the SiNPs were tested. When the fluorescence excitation peak was set to 440 nm, SiNPs showed a fluorescence emission peak at 549 nm. In addition, the peaks at about 250 nm and 450 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum were caused by the π-π* and n-π* transitions in the SiNPs structure. The peaks greater than 550 nm may be related to the surface transition of light-emitting groups in SiNPs (Fig. S2A) [33–36]. Among them, the absorption peak at about 450 nm was consistent with the fluorescence excitation wavelength of the SiNPs, which was also in line with the luminescence properties of nanomaterials. Furthermore, the CIE chromaticity of SiNPs was obtained [color coordinate (0.4008, 0.5702), tristimulus values (48, 69, 3.5), uniform color space (0.1773, 0.5676)], which also indicated that the SiNPs emitting yellow-green fluorescence were successfully preparation (Fig. S2B).

The stability of nanomaterials was a factor that significantly affects their application. Therefore, the performance of the SiNPs was evaluated. Figs. S3A–D shown that the fluorescence intensity of the SiNPs fluctuated negligibly under different pH, NaCl concentration, radiation and storage time, which verified that the SiNPs has superior pH stability, strong ionic strength resistance and excellent photobleaching resistance. At the same time, the fluorescence emission performance of the SiNPs in different solvents has also been tested. Table S1 and Fig. 4B manifested that the relative fluorescence intensity of SiNPs in different solvents was linearly related to the solvent polarity
E N T
. Fig. S4A demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the decrease of solvent polarity, which may be due to the vibration relaxation of internal conversion and fluorescence competition caused by the increase of hydrogen bond strength, resulting in the reduction of photons emitted by fluorescence [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and the formation mechanism of the SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the formation mechanism of the SiNPs, XPS was first used to reveal the structure or chemical bond information of the SiNPs. Fig. S5 illustrated that the SiNPs contained C, N, O, and Si elements. The fine spectrum of C1s in Fig. 1 A displayed the presence of C–Si (284.2 eV), C–C/CC (284.6 eV), C–N (285.1 eV), C–O/C–O–C (285.7 eV) in the SiNPs [38]. The existence of N–Si (398.8 eV) and C–N–C (399.4 eV) was proved by the N 1s fine spectrum (Fig. 1B) [39]. The fine spectrum of O 1s stated that the SiNPs contained O–Si (531.3 eV and 532.1 eV), O–C (532.7 eV) (Fig. 1C) [39]. The appearance of Si–C (101.7 eV), Si–N (102.1 eV) and Si–O (102.7 eV) was attested by the Si 2p fine spectrum (Fig. 1D) [40]. To corroborate the XPS results, the FTIR of SiNPs was also measured. The peaks at different wavenumbers [ν (N–H, O–H) = 3481-3266 cm−1, ν
as
(C–H) = ∼3012 cm−1, ν
s
(C–H) = ∼2790 cm−1, aromatic ring stretching vibration absorption bands = 1663-1453 cm−1, ν (C–N) = ∼1409 cm−1, ν (C–O) = ∼1303 cm−1, ν (Si–O–Si) = 1127-1033 cm−1, ν
s
(Si–N) = ∼927 cm−1] in Fig. 1E verified that the benzene ring structure in DAPH was retained in SiNPs [9,30,39,41]. It was also verified that SiNPs were rich in –OH and –NH2, which promoted SiNPs to have predominant water solubility and bioimaging performance. Moreover, the morphology and size of the SiNPs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 1F uncovered that the SiNPs possessed outstanding dispersibility, with a particle size distribution between 6.3 nm and 8.3 nm, and an average particle size of 7.3 nm. The peak at about 21° in Fig. S6 indicated that SiNPs have an amorphous structure, which was also consistent with the UV–Vis spectrum of SiNPs because the intensity of the UV–Vis absorption peak could explain the electron-hole recombination rate, the more lattice defects (the worse the crystallinity), the higher the peak height (the higher the recombination rate) [10]. The performance that the fluorescence emission wavelength of the SiNPs changed with the excitation wavelength in Fig. S7 also verified its uneven particle size.

By reason of the foregoing, SiNPs were mixtures formed by different degrees of polymerization of different DAPH and DAMO. Therefore, SiNPs may be formed through the following reaction process: First, the amino groups with smaller and larger steric hindrance on DAPH reacted with DAMO sequentially to form the structural unit shown in Fig. 2 . Subsequently, with the prolongation of the reaction time, the structural unit underwent different degrees of polymerization to form SiNPs that emit yellow-green fluorescence. The proposed mechanism was consistent with our previous reports [10]. To demonstrate the proposed mechanism, ultra high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS) was used to verify the composition of the SiNPs. As shown in Fig. S8, SiNPs mainly contained two components. Whereafter, the mass spectra of each component were analyzed. For peak 1 on the left of the chromatogram, its mass spectrum was shown in Fig. S9. The peak of m/z = 745 corresponded to the structure exhibited in Fig. S10 after the loss of fragments 1 and 2, which would form the structure displayed in Fig. S11 and Fig. S12 after further splitting. For peak 2 on the right of the chromatogram, its mass spectrum is manifested in Fig. S13. Among them, m/z = 997 corresponded to the structure shown in Fig. S14 after the loss of fragments 1 and 2. After the structure was further split, the structure exposed in Figs. S15–S18 will be formed. Therefore, SiNPs mainly consisted of the structures shown in Fig. S10 and Fig. S14. In other words, after varying degrees of polymerization, DAMO and DAPH mainly formed two structures as revealed in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of the SiNPs to NPs isomers
ce:section.ce:para:
As the above-mentioned fluorescence stability results, SiNPs revealed negligible difference in fluorescence intensity at pH 3.0–12.0. In addition, SiNPs evidenced discrepancy in fluorescence response to 500 μM NPs isomers, and the quenching efficiencies of 2-NP, 3-NP and 4-NP for SiNPs were 89.3%, 37.4% and 97.8%, respectively (Fig. S19). This response could reach equilibrium within 30 s (Fig. S20). Considering that NPs isomers existed commonly in abiotic tissues, pH 7.0 50 mM Tris-HCl was selected as the matrix for SiNPs to detect NPs isomers. As shown in Fig. 3 A–C, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased after adding 2-NP, 3-NP and 4-NP to SiNPs, respectively. Correspondingly, the log(F
0 /F) values of the SiNPs fluorescence intensity and 2-NP exhibited a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.999) between concentrations of 0.05–600 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) (3 s/k, in which s is the standard deviation for the blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve) was 16.7 nM (Fig. 3D). For 3-NP, the F
0 /F values of the SiNPs showed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.973) with concentrations of 3-NP between 20 and 600 μM, with a LOD of 6.7 μM (Fig. 3E). However, the log(F
0 /F) values of the SiNPs manifested a good linear relationship with 4-NP at concentrations of 0.01–600 μM (R2 = 0.983), with a LOD of 3.3 nM (Fig. 3F). These LODs were lower than the USA Environmental Protection Agency-recommended concentrations of 2-NP (0.55 μg/mL, 3.96 μM) and 4-NP (0.12 μg/mL, 0.86 μM) in fresh and saltwater [18]. Compared with the performance of reported methods for the detection of NPs isomers, the LOD of the SiNP-based fluorescence strategy was comparable to the others (Table 1 ) [18,19,27,28,42–49]. In summary, SiNPs showed different sensitivities to NPs isomers in the order 4-NP > 2-NP > 3-NP.

In addition, aromatic compounds structurally similar to the above three analytes (2-NP, 3-NP, 4-NP) were chosen as interfering substances to evaluate the selectivity of the method. As indicated in Fig. 3G–I, the fluorescence intensity of the SiNPs solution basically unchanged after adding the above substances (500 μM). Furthermore, while adding the mixed solution of 2-NP (or 3-NP, 4-NP) and these interfering substances to the SiNPs solution, the fluorescence intensity of the system was basically the same as that when only 2-NP (or 3-NP, 4-NP) was added. Likewise, the selectivity of the SiNPs for 4-NP has been evaluated at much lower concentration (1 μM) because of the present method displayed rather high selectivity for 4-NP, the obtained data were consistent with the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs with 500 μM interferents (Fig. S21). The above results disclosed that the method displayed excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability for the detection of 2-NP (or 3-NP, 4-NP), and were comparable to or even better than the previous reports [18,19,27,28,42–49].

To investigate the practical application ability of the fluorescence method established based on SiNPs, the Yellow River water samples were centrifuged (8000 r/min, 5 min) and the supernatant was collected, which was then filtered with a 0.1 μm membrane. Thereafter, the NPs isomers in the samples were determined by the standard addition method and their contents were calculated using the standard curve established above. Table 2 manifested that NPs isomers were undetected in the Yellow River water samples, indicating that the measures taken in recent years to protect the ecological environment of the Yellow River Basin have played a favorable role. The established fluorescence method exhibited good recovery results (99.5–109.0%) in the detection of NPs isomers in samples, revealing that the method disclosed good application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the detection mechanism of NPs isomers by SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the detection mechanism, the optical properties of the SiNPs and NPs isomers were measured. It can be seen from Fig. 4A–C that the UV–Vis absorption spectra of 2-NP (or 3-NP, 4-NP) and SiNPs displayed a large overlap area, respectively. In addition, the theoretical and experimental peak shapes of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the mixed solution of 2-NP (or 3-NP, 4-NP) and SiNPs were similar, but the intensity of the absorption peak was slightly different and no new absorption peak appears, which indicated that there was no new compounds was formed between the NPs isomers and SiNPs. Therefore, the phenomenon of 2-NP (or 3-NP, 4-NP)-induced fluorescence quenching of SiNPs may originate from the inner filter effect [50]. Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetime of SiNPs solution with 50 μM NPs was determined. Table S2 shown that NPs hardly changed the fluorescence lifetime of SiNPs, which further indicating that the quenching mechanism was inner filter effect rather than fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism. Furthermore, F 0/F = 0.954 + 6.97 × 10−4 [3-NP] and R2 = 0.973 for 3-NP (Fig. 3E), and F 0/F ≠ 1+ K SV [NPs] for 2-NP and 4-NP (Fig. S22), which were inconsistent in Stern-Volmer equation, representing that dynamic quenching and static quenching mechanisms could be ignored [41,50]. Therefore, the detection mechanism of NPs by SiNPs may be inner filter effect. In addition, Fig. 4D manifested that the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NPs isomers and the fluorescence excitation spectra of the SiNPs have different degrees of overlapping area (4-NP > 2-NP > 3-NP), these difference also indirectly indicated that the sensitivity of the SiNPs to detect NP isomers was 4-NP > 2-NP > 3-NP from high to low, which was consistent with the experimental results of this work. Most importantly, these overlapping results further demonstrated that the above-mentioned mechanism [10,51,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell activity and bioimaging of the SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The MTT method was used to evaluate the effect of the SiNPs on Hela cell viability, and the detailed procedures were consistent with our previous reports [10]. Fig. 5 affirmed that the cell activity was still greater than 85% even when the concentration of SiNPs was as high as 1000 μg/mL, indicating that the SiNPs had excellent biocompatibility. Then, the laser scanning confocal microscope was used to observe Hela cells incubated with SiNPs for 2 h. Fig. 5B–D demonstrated that the SiNPs could be internalized by Hela cells, showing that the SiNPs have excellent biological application potential and could be used as a stain for Hela cells.

In summary, SiNPs emitting yellow-green fluorescence were prepared by a one-step method at a mild room temperature. The obtained SiNPs have excellent pH stability, salt tolerance, photobleaching resistance and biocompatibility. In particular, through a series of characterization results, the formation mechanism of SiNPs was proposed, which provided a theoretical basis for the design and synthesis of SiNPs emitting different wavelengths of fluorescence, and also provided an important reference for the preparation of other types of nanomaterials. In addition, SiNP-based fluorescence method could realize the detection of 2-NP, 3-NP and 4-NP in the Yellow River water with satisfactory recoveries (99.5–109.0%). The method provided a valuable platform for the determination of residual NPs isomers in environmental samples.

Yangxia Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Data Curation, Validation, Writing-Original Draft. Yuxiang Wang: Formal analysis, Validation. Haixia Zhang: Validation, Supervision. Liang Zhao: Writing-review & editing, Supervision. Hongdeng Qiu: Writing-Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by CAS “light of West China” Program (No. E1YX027201); Gansu Youth Science and Technology Fund Project (21JR1RA352, 20JR10RA051); Gansu Innovation Group Project (20JR5RA573); and CAS-JSPS Cooperation and Exchange Program (No. E20489GHZ1).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical rotation and thermometry of laser tweezed silicon nanorods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Karpinski, S. Jones, H. Sipova-Jungova, R. Verre, M. Kall, Optical rotation and thermometry of laser tweezed silicon nanorods, Nano Lett. 20(9) (2020) 6494-6501. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.nanolett.0c02240

H. Mader, X. Li, S. Saleh, M. Link, P. Kele, O.S. Wolfbeis, Fluorescent silica nanoparticles, Ann. N.Y. Acad. Sci. 1130(1) (2008) 218-223. https://doi.org/10.1196/annals.1430.053

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A rapid and simple strategy for discrimination and detection of catechol and hydroquinone by fluorescent silicon nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yuan, B. Wang, C. Yan, W. Lv, Q. Ma, B. Zheng, J. Du, D. Xiao, A rapid and simple strategy for discrimination and detection of catechol and hydroquinone by fluorescent silicon nanoparticles, Microchem.
